Their music fit within the 'Countrypolitan' trend of the era, featuring slick production and Larry's soaring tenor vocals. They reached the pinnacle of the country charts as the decade closed with a landmark number-one hit.
key_songs_70s: I Just Wish You Were Someone I Love
trivia: Larry Gatlin won a Grammy Award for Best Country Song in 1976 for 'Broken Lady.'
The group was officially billed as Larry Gatlin & the Gatlin Brothers starting in 1979 after signing with Columbia Records.
Before their group success, Larry Gatlin worked as a background singer for Kris Kristofferson in Nashville.
youtube_search: Larry Gatlin All the Gold in California

name: Tom T. Hall
description: Nicknamed 'The Storyteller,' Tom T. Hall was one of the most acclaimed narrative songwriters of the 1970s. His sound was characterized by conversational lyrics and insightful observations of everyday life, resulting in a series of number-one hits. His unique ability to craft complex stories within country songs earned him widespread critical respect and a lasting legacy as a premier songwriter.
key_songs_70s: A Week in a Country Jail
The Year That Clayton Delaney Died
Faster Horses (The Cowboy and the Poet)
trivia: Hall was given his nickname 'The Storyteller' for his ability to compose songs about real lives and changing times.
His 1972 song '(Old Dogs, Children and) Watermelon Wine' is frequently cited as one of the greatest country songs ever written.
He won a Grammy Award for Best Album Notes in 1973 for the notes he wrote for his own 'Greatest Hits' album.

name: Townes Van Zandt
description: Townes Van Zandt was a definitive figure of the Texas progressive and outlaw country scenes in the 1970s. This decade was his most prolific, featuring the release of his most celebrated studio albums and signature songs like 'Pancho and Lefty.' His style was marked by melancholic, poetic lyrics and masterful fingerpicking, cementing his status as a legendary 'songwriter's songwriter.'

key_albums_70s.title: The Late Great Townes Van Zandt
Live at the Old Quarter, Houston, Texas

trivia: Van Zandt was featured prominently in the 1975 documentary 'Heartworn Highways' performing at his trailer home in Austin.
For much of the 1970s, he lived a reclusive life in a tin-roofed shack outside Nashville with no heat, plumbing, or telephone.
His signature song 'Pancho and Lefty' reached number one on the country charts for Willie Nelson and Merle Haggard in 1983.
youtube_search: Townes Van Zandt Pancho and Lefty

name: Vern Gosdin
description: After a brief retirement, Vern Gosdin returned to country music in the late 1970s and quickly earned the nickname 'The Voice.' His sound was deeply rooted in traditional country and showcased his powerful, emotional vocal range. He achieved his first string of top 10 solo hits during this period, often featuring lush harmony vocals from prominent artists like Emmylou Harris.
key_songs_70s: You've Got Me to Hold On To
trivia: Gosdin briefly retired from performing earlier in the 1970s to operate a glass company in Cartersville, Georgia.
His late 70s comeback hits frequently featured harmony vocals from Emmylou Harris and Janie Fricke.
He was widely known as 'The Voice' for his mastery of over-the-top emotionalism in traditional country singing.

name: Waylon Jennings
description: Waylon Jennings was a primary driving force behind the outlaw country movement in the 1970s. After winning creative control at RCA, he developed a powerful sound characterized by rock-influenced rhythms and his signature 'spanky-twang' guitar style. He released a series of landmark albums that rejected the Nashville sound in favor of a raw, authentic approach that defined a decade.

key_albums_70s.title: Are You Ready for the Country

key_songs_70s: Are You Sure Hank Done It This Way
Mammas Don't Let Your Babies Grow Up to Be Cowboys
trivia: In 1976, Jennings co-led 'Wanted! The Outlaws,' which became the first country music album ever to be certified platinum.
He was arrested by federal agents in his recording studio in 1977, an event he later memorialized in his hit 'Don't You Think This Outlaw Bit's Done Got Out of Hand.'
He famously gave up his seat on the 1959 plane flight that killed Buddy Holly, an event that profoundly shaped his later life.

name: Webb Pierce
description: While his greatest commercial dominance was in the 1950s, Webb Pierce remained a visible traditional country figure throughout the 1970s. He continued to release studio albums and singles on the Decca and Plantation labels, maintaining his signature honky tonk sound. During this era, he became equally famous for his flamboyant lifestyle, including silver-dollar lined convertibles and a guitar-shaped swimming pool.

key_albums_70s.title: Love Ain't Never Gonna Be No Better

key_songs_70s: The Man You Want Me to Be
Tell Him That You Love Him
The Good Lord Giveth (And Uncle Sam Taketh Away)
I've Got Leaving on My Mind
trivia: Pierce built a $30,000 guitar-shaped swimming pool at his Nashville home that became a massive tourist attraction until his neighbors sued.
He was known for his flamboyant Nudie suits and silver dollar-lined convertibles, defining country music excess in the 70s.
He was the first country performer to have a successful single that featured a pedal steel guitar.
youtube_search: Webb Pierce In the Jailhouse Now

name: Willie Nelson
description: Willie Nelson became a towering figure of the outlaw country movement in the 1970s after moving to Austin and seeking creative freedom. His sound, a unique blend of traditional country with folk and jazz influences, was showcased on landmark albums like 'Red Headed Stranger' and 'Stardust.' This decade saw him transition from a songwriter for others into one of the most recognized and acclaimed performers in American music.

key_albums_70s.title: The Words Don't Fit the Picture

key_songs_70s: Blue Eyes Crying in the Rain
If You've Got the Money I've Got the Time
trivia: Nelson purchased his famous Martin N-20 guitar, 'Trigger,' unseen for $750 in 1969 after his previous guitar was damaged.
He established his annual 'Fourth of July Picnic' festival in 1973 after the failure of the Dripping Springs Reunion.
His move to Austin in 1972 rejuvenated his career and made him the leading figure of the Texas-based 'outlaw' scene.
youtube_search: Willie Nelson Blue Eyes Crying in the Rain
